mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage security, compliance, and governance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce policies without needing to learn a new language. Key trade-offs include the need for careful policy design to avoid unintended consequences and the potential complexity of managing policies at scale.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository for Helm.

steps.title: Install Kyverno
steps.description:
This command installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace using the specified version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Create a namespace for Kyverno.
> Install Kyverno with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This command checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Ensure all Kyverno pods are in the 'Running' state.

steps.title: Create an example policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/main/docs/examples/policies/policy.yaml

```

> Apply an example policy to the cluster.

steps.title: Verify the policy
steps.description:
This command checks if the policy has been applied successfully.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -n kyverno

```

> List the custom policies to verify the example policy is present.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all policies and configurations managed by Kyverno.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstall the Kyverno release.
> Delete the Kyverno namespace and all its resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your policies. This step involves upgrading to a newer version of Kyverno.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -n kyverno -o yaml > kyverno-policies-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backup existing policies.
> Upgrade to the specified version.
> Verify that all pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get cpol -n kyverno -o yaml > kyverno-policies-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Kyverno is not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kyverno pods may not have started due to resource constraints or misconfiguration.

**Fix:**
Check pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' and ensure sufficient resources are allocated.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Policy failed to apply'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy may have syntax errors or conflicts with existing resources.

**Fix:**
Validate the policy syntax and check for conflicts with 'kubectl describe cpol <policy-name> -n kyverno'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Helm install fails with version conflict'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified version of Kyverno may not be compatible with your Kubernetes version.

**Fix:**
Check compatibility and install a supported version using 'helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --version <compatible-version>'.

**Affected versions:** <= 1.29

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No policies found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Policies may not have been applied or were deleted.

**Fix:**
Reapply the policies using 'kubectl apply -f <policy-file.yaml>'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new policy types and improved validation mechanisms.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Review and update existing policies to align with the new format.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno' showing all pods in 'Running' state.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25 or higher and the necessary tools installed.
